name: Cross-Service Timeout / Retry Amplification
incident_description: Multiple services are returning 503 errors. Started 20 minutes ago. The billing service is responding slowly (p99 latency jumped from 200ms to 8s), and downstream services are timing out and retrying, causing cascading failures. The system was stable until a configuration change was applied to the billing service's database connection pool.

services.name: notification-service
services.role: Email/SMS notifications, calls billing-service for receipts

recent_changes.description: Reduced DB connection pool from 50 to 5 connections to 'save resources' per ops team request

traces.failing_request: api-gateway (2ms) → order-service (5ms) → billing-service (3000ms TIMEOUT) → retry → billing-service (3000ms TIMEOUT) → retry → billing-service (3000ms TIMEOUT) → 503

root_cause: Ops team reduced billing-service DB connection pool from 50 to 5 connections. Under normal load, billing-service needs ~30 concurrent DB connections. With only 5, requests queue behind the pool. This causes billing-service latency to spike to 8s (waiting for pool). Order-service has a 3s timeout with 3 retries, so each failed request generates 3 more requests to billing-service, amplifying the load 3×. Notification-service adds another 2× amplification. The retry storm makes the pool exhaustion worse, creating a positive feedback loop.

red_herrings.description: billing-service version 2.4.1 was deployed last week
red_herrings.why_misleading: Not recent; the config change was 20 minutes ago
It's a victim, not the cause — it's just reporting upstream failures
Effect, not cause — the 47 queued queries are consuming CPU. Pool was the trigger.
